title: Activation energy
summary: 9701 AS kinetics — activation energy, Boltzmann distribution, and effect of temperature on rate with PhET States of Matter Basics.
learningObjectives: Define activation energy Ea as the minimum energy for a successful collision.
Explain why rate increases with temperature using collision theory.
Sketch Boltzmann distributions at two temperatures.
Explain how catalysts lower Ea without changing ΔH.

simpleExplanation.title: The energy hurdle
simpleExplanation.summary: Most collisions don't react — particles need enough kinetic energy to overcome the activation energy barrier. Heating increases the fraction of high-energy collisions dramatically.
steps: Draw Boltzmann curve: many particles with moderate energy, few with very high energy.
Shade area above Ea — this fraction reacts (approx proportional to rate).
Higher T shifts curve right and spreads it — more particles exceed Ea.
Catalyst lowers Ea — same ΔH, different pathway; more collisions succeed.

sections.content: **Activation energy** links Topic 8 (kinetics) to Topic 5 (energetics). Catalysts change **Ea**, not **ΔH** — a favourite Cambridge distinction.

items: Boltzmann: axes = number of particles vs kinetic energy
Higher T: peak shifts right, curve lower and broader
Area under curve above Ea ∝ rate (qualitative argument)
Homogeneous catalyst same phase; heterogeneous on surface

sections.content: When sketching Boltzmann curves, label **both** curves (T₁ and T₂), mark **Ea** with a vertical line, and shade the area representing particles with E ≥ Ea.
